item.title: Does geo-located social media reflect the visit frequency of urban parks? A city-wide analysis using the count and content of photographs
PhD Defence: Exploring big data applications for urban greening
item.description:
Summary
Research on urban greening is inherently multi-disciplinary. Plants and green spaces in cities are part of a complex interplay of environmental and social factors, and have the potential to moderate the negative impacts of urbanisation. In recent years, vast quantities of electronic data—often referred to as ‘big data’—offer new opportunities to extract insights about our environment and human activities. This has generated much enthusiasm in the industry and research community, but the application of such methods to urban greening has been relatively slow. This talk explores the use of novel data and techniques for understanding the social, economic and ecological impacts of urban greening. Two main topics are examined: the performance of urban trees using data from tree inventories; and preferences for outdoor recreation based on the count and content of social media photographs. The potential and shortcomings of applying such techniques to urban greening practice will be discussed.

Supervisors:
Associate Prof. Dr Tan Puay Yok (National University of Singapore)
Prof. Dr Peter J. Edwards (ETH Zurich)


Time tags

Presentation:
0:18 Introduction
4:26 Topic 1: Performance of city trees using data from tree inventories
14:57 Topic 2: Preferences for outdoor recreation using social media photographs
26:09 Synthesis of results: Applications to urban greening

Q&A:
40:50 Examiner 1: Associate Prof. Dr Enrico Di Minin (University of Helsinki)
1:11:17 Examiner 2: Assistant Prof. Dr Chao Yuan (National University of Singapore)
1:21:02 Examiner 3: Assistant Prof. Dr Ye Zhang (National University of Singapore)
1:39:13 Conclusion

item.title: Exploring big data applications for urban greening
Social media, nature, and life satisfaction: global evidence of the biophilia hypothesis
Analysing spatial patterns of the landscape
item.description:
In landscape ecology, we study interactions between organisms and their surrounding environment. The physical landscape is often quantified using “landscape metrics”, for further analyses alongside other sources of data.

My recent lectures are focused on introducing such concepts to landscape architects. While I’ve used FRAGSTATS and other GIS software for teaching in the past, I’ve always struggled with compatibility/licensing issues that just made the whole process really cumbersome.

Recently, I came across this R package called landscapemetrics. I was absolutely blown away by the sheer power of this thing. I’ve always wanted to introduce people to spatial analysis in R, and to show how a seamless, reproducible workflow in R can be an excellent alternative to conventional software. This was the tipping point for me… I decided to give it a shot!

This post provides a quick preview of the workshop content. The workshop is meant to be a follow-up lesson to the Introduction to R series I’ll be conducting next semester.

Workshop outline:


  1. Why analyse spatial patterns?

  2. Landscape ecology: Conceptual models

  3. Land cover classification

  4. Landscape metrics


I’ve uploaded the slides and data on Github, so that anyone interested to try out the analysis for themselves will be able to do so. If you’re interested in geospatial analysis, check it out!



Slide deck:

item.title: Using social media user attributes to understand human–environment interactions at urban parks
Outdoor Recreation in Cities
item.description:
Parks and green spaces are important places for outdoor recreation in cities. They also provide opportunities for city dwellers to experience nature, and contribute to health and well-being in numerous ways.

People’s experiences of their surroundings can vary depending on where they are and their personal interests. Today, the internet and geo-located social media have opened up new ways for us to understand these differences. Technologies to interpret text and image content have also been improving, which can be used to assess the effects of the environment on people’s behaviour, sentiment and aesthetic experiences at specific places. Many studies have used geo-located social media as a proxy for outdoor recreation, but such data are often generated bottom-up, making it challenging to ensure that all people groups are properly represented.

There is also a need to ensure that people have equitable access to a variety of parks from their homes. This requires effective ways to measure the spatial provision of parks, alongside the expected demand for park use. Ongoing work seeks to integrate these techniques with park planning and design.


Variation across parks in Singapore according to the kinds of social media users they attract. User groups were based on (a) residency status, as well as the relative amounts of (b) Landscapes–People and (c) Wildlife–City content within favourited photographs. Figure from Song et al. (2020).


Opportunities

We offer projects for motivated students interested in this topic, depending on availability per semester. Prospective students may contact me via email.
